subject: [PATCH] fs/super: skip non-memcg-aware nr_cached_objects in memcg slab shrink

reviews.author: Jan Kara
reviews.summary: Reviewer Jan Kara expressed surprise that the XFS inode shrinker is not memcg-aware, but agreed with the patch change; he also suggested notifying the XFS developers about this.
reviews.raw_body:
To me this makes sense. However I'm bit surprised that XFS inode shrinker
(which is what gets counted in nr_cached_objects for XFS) isn't memcg
aware. I guess since these inodes are on their way to a relatively quick
destruction, nobody really bothered. So I'm fine with the change, just
I'd like to make sure XFS folks are aware and don't plan anything in this
area.

								Honza

reviews.author: Usama Arif (author)
reviews.summary: Author acknowledged that feedback on the patch is needed from xfs and btrfs maintainers, so they can raise any objections before a potential revision.
reviews.raw_body:
Thanks for the review!

Yes I am hoping that if there are any objections from xfs or btrfs, it gets
raised. Have cc'ed the btrfs and xfs maintainers and reviewers.

Thanks!
Usama

2026-06-09.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's super_block shrinker to skip non-memcg-aware nr_cached_objects hooks during per-memcg slab reclaim. The issue arises when memcg-heavy slab reclaim is performed, causing hundreds of duplicate work items to be queued and resulting in wasted CPU cycles. To address this, the patch restricts the invocation of these global fs-specific hooks to only occur on the root/global shrink path, ensuring that the actual reclaim happens on the global path rather than per-memcg.

reviews.author: Christian Brauner
reviews.summary: The reviewer noted that the patch restricts ->nr_cached_objects() to the global shrink path, but this may not be sufficient as it does not address the issue of duplicate work being queued from per-memcg shrink_slab_memcg() invocations.
reviews.raw_body:
Applied to the vfs-7.3.misc branch of the vfs/vfs.git tree.
Patches in the vfs-7.3.misc branch should appear in linux-next soon.

Please report any outstanding bugs that were missed during review in a
new review to the original patch series allowing us to drop it.

It's encouraged to provide Acked-bys and Reviewed-bys even though the
patch has now been applied. If possible patch trailers will be updated.

Note that commit hashes shown below are subject to change due to rebase,
trailer updates or similar. If in doubt, please check the listed branch.

tree:   https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/vfs/vfs.git
branch: vfs-7.3.misc

[1/1] fs/super: skip non-memcg-aware nr_cached_objects in memcg slab shrink
      https://git.kernel.org/vfs/vfs/c/5eacee52be6b

reviews.author: Shakeel Butt
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Shakeel Butt questioned whether the patch would cause unreclaimable memory in a rare scenario where memcg-charged objects tracked by ->nr_cached_objects() become stuck, requesting consideration of this edge case.

Reviewer Shakeel Butt questioned whether the patch's solution addresses the correct problem, asking if it's about wasting CPU or over-reclaiming/reclaiming from unrelated memcgs

Reviewer Shakeel Butt suggested that proper memcg awareness should be implemented in xfs and shmem callbacks, and expressed uncertainty about whether btrfs_extent_map objects should be charged to memcg.
reviews.raw_body:
I see the underlying objects whose count is returned by ->nr_cached_objects()
hook is memcg charged for shmem and xfs but not for btrfs. Do you envision
there might be a rare scenario where we have a lot of memory charged to a memcg
consumed by objects which ->nr_cached_objects() tracks and that memory becomes
unreclaimable due to this patch?

---

What is the main concern? Is it the amount of CPU wasted or are we over
reclaiming or reclaiming from unrelated memcgs?

---

I am fine with the stopgap but it would be nice to have proper memcg awareness
in xfs and shmem callbacks. For btrfs, I am not sure if it makes sense to memcg
charge btrfs_extent_map objects but at least to decision to skip memcg reclaim
will be inside the fs callbacks i.e. nr_cached_objects.

2026-06-23.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's super_block shrinker to skip non-memcg-aware nr_cached_objects hooks during per-memcg slab reclaim. The issue arises when memcg-heavy slab reclaim is performed, causing hundreds of duplicate work items to be queued and resulting in wasted CPU cycles. To address this, the patch restricts the invocation of these global fs-specific hooks to only occur on the root/global shrink path, ensuring that the actual reclaim happens on the global path rather than per-memcg.
